factual-accuracy.requirement: States individualism scores: South Korea collectivist (IDV 18±5), Finland individualist (IDV 63±5), Mexico collectivist (IDV 30±5)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states Hofstede individualism (IDV) scores for South Korea as ~18, Finland as ~63, and Mexico as ~30, and characterizes South Korea and Mexico as collectivist and Finland as individualist/moderately individualist.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States power distance scores: South Korea mid-high (PDI 60±10), Finland low (PDI 33±5), Mexico very high (PDI 81±10)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states South Korea’s PDI as ~60 (moderate-high), Finland’s PDI as ~33 (low), and Mexico’s PDI as ~81 (very high), matching the required mid-high/low/very high ranges.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States uncertainty avoidance: South Korea very high (UAI 85±10), Finland moderate (UAI 59±10), Mexico high (UAI 82±10)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states uncertainty avoidance scores for all three countries: South Korea UAI ~85, Finland UAI ~59, and Mexico UAI ~82. These values match the required “very high/moderate/high” characterizations and fall within the specified ±10 ranges.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes Classting as teacher-moderated or teacher-led class SNS with private counseling rooms/secret channels and coarse moderation controls
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Classting as a teacher-mediated platform and explicitly mentions a “Private Counseling” feature with optional student anonymity in one-on-one conversations. It also repeatedly frames Classting collaboration as teacher-controlled/teacher-led rather than peer-led, aligning with coarse teacher moderation expectations.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Wilma has 95-98% coverage in Finnish basic education schools and handles attendance/grades/messaging
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Wilma is used in more than 95% of Finnish primary and secondary schools and describes it as handling communication/messaging plus viewing schedules/homework/exams, absence management, and test results/grades. This satisfies the required coverage range (95–98%) and the attendance/grades/messaging functions.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions Google Classroom Mexico/Latin America deployments reached state scale (hundreds of thousands to 1M+ students) with private comments and teacher-controlled posting emphasized
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Google Classroom Latin America deployments at large scale (e.g., Jalisco 1.3 million students) and discusses private/anonymous feedback via third-party tools, but it does not mention “state scale” explicitly nor emphasize Google Classroom’s native private comments and teacher-controlled posting features as key deployment practices.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States anonymous peer assessment increases cognitive/higher-order feedback or improves final grades significantly versus identified review
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly claims anonymous peer review yields more critical/higher-order feedback than identified review (e.g., “2.8x more critical comments,” “45% more critical feedback,” and “more feedback on higher-order concerns”) and also reports higher writing achievement/posttest scores for anonymous conditions versus identifiable groups.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States anonymous question queues or LAQQ reduce apprehension and increase question submission versus public-only channels
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response repeatedly states that anonymous peer review/feedback increases participation and critical feedback (e.g., “anonymity increases participation quantity,” “anonymous feedback produces 2.8x more critical comments,” and recommendations for double-blind anonymity to help reluctant students speak up). These claims directly assert that anonymous mechanisms reduce apprehension and increase question/feedback submission versus identified/public-only channels.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains that high power distance reduces willingness to publicly challenge peers or request help in digital learning environments
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly links higher power distance to reluctance to challenge peers publicly (e.g., noting students are “reluctant to challenge peers' work publicly” in Korea and that Mexico’s high PDI suppresses public critique). It also explains that high power distance reduces communication/help-seeking due to fear of authority, and highlights anonymous/private channels as a workaround.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends masked/anonymous early drafts, teacher-approved public showcases, anonymous live Q&A, or teacher-moderated boards for Korean group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends anonymity and teacher moderation for Korea, including “anonymous double-blind option,” “anonymous-to-peers, known-to-teacher,” and “teacher approves…comment review,” which align with masked early drafts and moderated showcases/boards. It also proposes anonymous idea submission and structured turn-taking to reduce hierarchy effects in Korean contexts.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends named edits/contributions, visible cursors, open collaboration, or student self-organization for Finnish group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends Finnish workspace features including full named edit histories with student access, full real-time presence cues (avatars/cursors) with opt-out, and emphasizes student agency/self-organization in Finland’s design principles. These are explicitly presented in the Finland section’s recommended feature configuration.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends private-first design, mobile/offline editing, teacher-controlled visibility, or teacher-only posting initially for Mexican group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends Mexico-specific defaults including asynchronous/mobile-first and offline-capable design, plus teacher-centric controls such as “teacher-first deployment,” “teacher moderation: high control,” and “named edit histories visible to teacher only.” It also suggests double-blind anonymity and teacher-only visibility options for Mexican group workspaces.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends double-blind or anonymous peer review with rubric-guided stems or structured feedback prompts for Korea
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends for South Korea a structured peer review workflow with “anonymous double-blind option” and “anonymous-to-peers, known-to-teacher” identity, explicitly tied to rubric-based templates and structured feedback prompts in the Korea recommendations section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends named peer review with feedback literacy scaffolding, exemplars, or process-focused critique for Finland
factual-accuracy.explanation: For Finland, the response recommends an identified (named) peer review workflow and explicitly mentions optional rubrics and other scaffolding (e.g., “optional rubrics,” “phenomenon-based learning templates”). It also emphasizes formative, process-oriented feedback and multiple peer feedback, which aligns with process-focused critique scaffolding.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends two-tier identity (anonymous to peers, identified to teacher) or private-first peer review with structured stems for Mexico
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends for Mexico a double-blind/two-tier identity approach where peers are anonymous but the teacher can access real identities, and it also calls for structured, rubric-based peer review workflows (i.e., structured stems/templates).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Claims Finland has higher power distance than Korea or Mexico, or claims Mexico has lower power distance than Finland
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states Finland has low power distance (PDI ~33) while Korea is moderate-high (~60) and Mexico is very high (~81). It does not claim Finland has higher power distance than Korea or Mexico, nor that Mexico has lower power distance than Finland.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Predicts anonymity or power-distance interventions will show stronger effects in high-PDI markets (Korea/Mexico) versus low-PDI (Finland)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly predicts anonymity and higher teacher moderation will be more beneficial in high power-distance contexts (South Korea and Mexico) than in low-PDI Finland, stating anonymity yields larger gains in critical feedback and participation for Korea/Mexico while being less necessary/impactful in Finland.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Describes temporal rollout strategies (private-first then gradual opening) with triggers or norms that enable stage transitions
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response describes staged deployment norms such as “teacher-first deployment” and “gradual release of responsibility,” and recommends defaults like asynchronous-first with synchronous optional. However, it does not specify concrete temporal rollout stages (e.g., private beta → pilot → public) nor explicit triggers/thresholds (metrics or events) that determine when to transition between stages.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies negative consequences of recommended patterns (e.g., fairness concerns) and proposes compensating design elements or safeguards
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response flags negative consequences of recommended patterns—especially anonymity increasing incivility and reducing trust, and transparency/version history creating surveillance or draft anxiety—and then proposes safeguards like teacher moderation, AI filtering, two-tier identity (anonymous-to-peers/known-to-teacher), optional anonymity, and configurable presence cues.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies measurable outcomes beyond raw counts (higher-order comments, revision adoption rates) with expected effect ranges from research
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response specifies measurable outcomes beyond raw counts, including higher-order feedback incidence (e.g., 45% more critical comments; 2.8x more critical comments), revision adoption rates (~47% incorporated), and effect sizes (e.g., g=0.833, d=1.23), and it frames these as expected ranges/effects from research.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects technical constraints (offline capability, bandwidth limits) to required collaboration design patterns in specific markets
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly maps technical constraints like offline capability, bandwidth limits, device access, and smartphone bans to collaboration design patterns (e.g., async-first, lightweight text/audio, feature degradation, offline sync, limiting real-time presence cues) for South Korea, Finland, and Mexico. It does this in dedicated sections and market-specific recommendation tables tying infrastructure to collaboration features.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Draws explicit connections between existing platform capabilities (private channels, moderation toggles) and cultural requirements they address
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly links platform features to cultural needs, e.g., Classting’s anonymous “Private Counseling” to Korean face-saving, Google Docs version history/presence cues to Mexico’s high power distance dynamics, and Finland’s identified communication norms to Wilma’s non-anonymous design. It also recommends configurable teacher moderation and anonymity as culturally adaptive defaults tied to hierarchy and uncertainty avoidance.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Notes contexts where cultural predictions don't hold or explicitly flags inferences versus direct empirical evidence
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly flags limits and tradeoffs in cultural generalizations (e.g., noting anonymity benefits are moderated by educational level and can reduce trust, and that effects vary by condition). It also distinguishes evidence types by citing specific studies and effect sizes versus broader Hofstede-based implications, implying which claims are inference-driven versus empirically measured.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides identical design recommendations for all three markets without adapting interaction patterns to their different PDI or collectivism profiles
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response differentiates recommendations by market, explicitly tailoring anonymity, teacher moderation, presence cues, and workflow structure to South Korea, Finland, and Mexico based on their differing PDI/collectivism and infrastructure contexts. It proposes identified feedback for Finland but double-blind anonymity and higher teacher control for Korea/Mexico, so it is not identical across all three.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis by feature or market with consistent comparative structure (e.g., Korea/Finland/Mexico breakdowns per feature)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is consistently organized by market (South Korea, Finland, Mexico) and repeats the same comparative feature structure across sections (e.g., platform implementation subsections and interaction-pattern tables for each market). It also provides per-market “Recommended Feature Configuration” tables using the same feature rows (peer review, version control, presence cues, identity, moderation), enabling direct comparison.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise UX/product design terminology (interaction patterns, moderation controls, engagement metrics, feature toggles)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses precise UX/product terminology throughout, including interaction patterns (presence indicators, version control, two-tier identity), moderation controls (teacher approvals, comment review, flagged content), engagement metrics (participation rates, revision adoption rates, effect sizes), and feature toggles/optional settings (optional real-time, opt-out presence cues, anonymous feedback toggles).

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective, research-oriented tone connecting design recommendations to empirical findings without marketing claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, analytical tone and repeatedly ties interface recommendations (e.g., anonymity, moderation, presence cues) to cited studies and reported effect sizes/metrics. It avoids promotional language about the product and frames claims as research-backed implications rather than marketing promises.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents engagement effects with specific metrics or ranges (e.g., percentage increases, effect sizes) rather than only qualitative descriptions
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes multiple quantitative engagement-related metrics and effect sizes (e.g., g = 0.833, d = 1.23, 2.8x more critical comments, 45% more negative comments, ~47% feedback incorporation, and 10–20 percentage-point participation differences) rather than relying only on qualitative descriptions.

presentation-quality.requirement: Restates the same cultural insight or design recommendation multiple times using different wording without adding new analysis
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly reiterates the same core recommendations—use anonymity for high power-distance/collectivist contexts, use structured rubrics for high uncertainty avoidance, and increase teacher moderation in Korea/Mexico—across the executive summary, platform sections, metrics sections, and the final recommendations tables. These repetitions are often rephrased without adding materially new analysis each time.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Hofstede cultural dimensions framework for individualism and power distance scores across the three countries
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references Hofstede’s cultural dimensions and provides both Individualism (IDV) and Power Distance (PDI) scores for South Korea (IDV ~18, PDI ~60), Finland (IDV ~63, PDI ~33), and Mexico (IDV ~30, PDI ~81), citing Hofstede Insights among its sources.

citation-quality.requirement: References official documentation or deployment reports for all 3 platforms: Classting, Wilma, and Google Classroom deployments
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes citations to official or deployment documentation for Classting (e.g., Classting Support link), Wilma (e.g., Visma press release and municipal Wilma instructions), and Google Classroom Latin America deployments (e.g., Google for Education LATAM case study and Google Mexico blog). These references cover all three platforms as required.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites at least 2 academic sources on anonymous peer assessment, peer review, or participation mechanisms in educational contexts
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple academic sources on anonymous peer assessment/peer review, including Panadero & Alqassab (2019) on anonymity effects in peer assessment and the NCOLR (2007) study comparing anonymous versus identifiable e-peer review, along with additional peer feedback studies (e.g., Cho & MacArthur, 2010; Wu & Schunn, 2020).

citation-quality.requirement: Includes K-12 or secondary education research on cultural differences, engagement patterns, or digital collaboration
citation-quality.explanation: The response discusses secondary/K-12 contexts across South Korea, Finland, and Mexico and cites education research on cultural differences (e.g., power distance, collectivism), peer feedback/anonymity, and collaboration features affecting engagement and participation.

citation-quality.requirement: Uses Reddit, teacher forums, or social media as primary evidence for behavioral patterns or platform effectiveness
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites academic studies, official reports, and platform documentation as evidence. It does not use Reddit, teacher forums, or social media as primary evidence for behavioral patterns or platform effectiveness.
